---
title: "Session 10 · Model Building & Diagnostics"
---

## Overview

How do we decide which predictors belong in a model, and how do we know the fitted model can be trusted? This capstone of the linear half draws a sharp line between confirmatory model building, where theory dictates the specification, and exploratory model building, where the data guide it. We then turn to diagnostics --- examining residuals, checking the assumptions that license our inferences, and identifying influential observations that can quietly distort the entire fit.

## Learning objectives

By the end of this session, you will be able to:

- Distinguish confirmatory from exploratory approaches to model building.
- Select and compare candidate models using principled criteria.
- Examine residual plots to assess linearity, constant variance, and normality.
- Verify the assumptions that underlie regression inference.
- Detect influential observations and high-leverage points and assess their impact.

## This session's materials

- {{< fa book-open-reader >}} **Read:** [Chapter 7: Model Building](/notes/chapters/07-model-building.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa book-open-reader >}} **Read:** [Chapter 8: Model Diagnostics](/notes/chapters/08-model-diagnostics.html){target="_blank"}
- {{< fa laptop-code >}} **Lab:** [Lab 8: Model Building & Diagnostics](/labs/labs/lab08-diagnostics.html){target="_blank"}

## Key concepts

| Concept | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Confirmatory model building** | Specifying a model in advance from theory or hypotheses, before examining the data. |
| **Exploratory model building** | Using the data to search among candidate specifications for a model that fits well. |
| **Regression diagnostics** | Procedures for checking whether a fitted model satisfies its assumptions. |
| **Residual** | The difference between an observed outcome and its fitted value. |
| **Model assumptions** | The conditions --- linearity, constant variance, independence, normality --- that justify regression inference. |
| **Influential observation** | A data point that, if removed, would substantially change the estimated model. |
| **Leverage** | A measure of how far a point's predictor values lie from the rest, indicating its potential to sway the fit. |
